translation.content: If singeli has a new era, DJ Travella is its leading light. At just 23 years old, the Tanzanian producer is pushing the genre into fast, frenetic and unmistakably futuristic territory. And while there aren't too many entries in the RA Podcast's 20-year history where you can say, "this has no parallel whatsoever," RA.984 shatters that assumption in style. Singeli emerged from Dar es Salaam's underground in the early '00s, forged from limited resources and unlimited creativity. Producers looped and sped up taarab instrumentals using basic software like Virtual DJ, creating a sound that was chaotic, witty and lightning fast. With support from local studios like Sisso and Pamoja, singeli took root as the breakneck pulse of Tanzanian youth culture. Travella—real name Hamadi Hassani—came up outside that infrastructure. He began producing music aged ten, self-taught and internet-savvy. By 2022, he was touring Europe with Kampala-based collective Nyege Nyege and gaining global attention for a distinct style he's dubbed "cyber-singeli." Unbound by tradition, he takes the sound's basic structure and rockets it into something truly supersonic: just listen to the gleaming, 170-BPM euphoria of "Trust," from his latest EP, Twende, by way of proof. European fast club music has been catching heat for falling off lately. But like gabber, hardcore and jungle before it, singeli is unapologetically go hard or go home. It's unique and utterly infectious. After all, what could possibly connect pop provocateur Arca to the late president of Tanzania? Not much—except singeli. When Arca opened her Caracas Boiler Room set with "Crazy Beat Music Umeme 1," from Travella's breakout album Mr Mixondo, she tapped into the same breakneck energy that former president John Magufuli once used to soundtrack his campaigns and public appearances. Like many producers of his generation, Travella's creative process is deeply online. He scouts new tracks on Instagram, follows them down rabbit holes on YouTube and pulls influences from everywhere—no genre is off limits. His DJ sets and productions reflect that openness, bending singeli into thrilling new shapes. Travella's RA Podcast is a white-knuckle ride through this blistering sonic universe. It's wild and joyful yet controlled—a window into one of the most exciting young minds in global club music. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I work on new music every day and am constantly looking for some new sounds, mixing style and getting inspired from as many genres as possible, while keeping the identity of my own sound. I'm currently working on my next album and collaborating with other big artists such as Arca, Rosa Pistola and Freebot. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix was recorded in the Dar Es Salaam region, at Mr Mixondo's studio. What are you looking forward to in the near future? In the future, I hope singeli will become more popular and I believe that this music will reach more people and they will love it as much as the people who love it right now. I'm excited about being able to help that music expand and reach further and further. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I really like listening to other DJs, getting inspired and seeing what they do, creatively and technically so that I can try to do something bigger. Some artists who inspired me lately: Sisso, Bamba Pana, both from the singeli scene in Dar Es Salaam. Also GREG and Rosa Pistola. For people who might be new to your sound—how would you describe singeli in one sentence? Singeli is music to forget your problems, to feel good and happy, that's why it's fast and joyful. Singeli is so rooted in Dar es Salaam, but your music also feels experimental. How do you balance tradition with innovation? Singeli is our music, it's the music of the people and the music of the streets—so maybe it sounds experimental for people who are not from our culture, but at home it feels normal. I think my style is experimental because I'm always looking to develop the sound and the genre, also trying to see what works with different audiences around the world who are not familiar with the sound. I keep my tradition because of who I am, but I like to innovate because I want to keep inspiring people and test develop my style. If you don't change it's boring and I want people to hear my singeli like it's travella's singeli—that's my touch. Who’s inspiring you right now—whether that's other Singeli artists or something totally outside of it? I get inspired by artists I've met through my music, people like Arca and Bjork because their world is really their own, they take music to new limits... I'm also inspired by other genres of fast music, like bouyon, kuduro, balani of my friend DJ Diaki ...and of course, within our community. We always push—I love how Sisso and Maiko are doing their thing live with keyboards, and Jay Mitta for always challenging the sound and making it very, very hard!
translation.blurb: A mix beamed in from the future by singeli's young star.
